content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
. 7 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
December 2, 2015 
Joan A. Borger 
City Attorney 
456 W. Olive Avenue 
P O  Box 3707 
Sunnyvale, CA 94088 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-226 
Dear Ms. Borger: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Sunnyvale City Councilmember 
Gustav Larsson regarding conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
Under the Act, may Councilmember Larsson participate in decisions related to timing and 
phasing of construction, construction financing, extension of time frames, and ownership structure 
of housing units of the Sunnyvale Town Center Project ('"Town Center Project") located in Block 
18 of the Downtown Sunnyvale Specific Plan ("DSP")? 
CONCLUSION 
Councilmember Larsson may participate in decisions related to timing and phasing of 
construction of the Town Center Project so long as these decisions do not have a foreseeable 
material financial effect on his financial interests, and so long as they do not reopen, determine, 
affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions approving the DSP, the development plan for Block 
18, or any other decision from which he may be disqualified. 
Construction financing, extension of time frames, and possibly ownership structure of the 
housing units are decisions "inextricably interrelated" to the DSP and Block 18 determinations to 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8l000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 10 through 189 97 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-15-226 
Page No. 2 
the extent that they reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter these previous decisions. 
Therefore, Councilmember Larsson will be required to disqualify from these decisions. 
FACTS 
The Town Center Project is a project to complete construction of a mixed-use 
development consisting of retail, restaurant, entertainment, office, hotel and high-density 
residential units in Block 18 of the DSP. 
The City of Sunnyvale originally adopted its DSP in 1993. It was substantially revised 
in 2003. It comprises roughly 125 acres, which are subdivided into blocks. Each block has its 
own development standards and regulations. Block 18 is located on the site of the former 
Town Center Mall. It is a redevelopment plan area of the fonner Sunnyvale Redevelopment 
Agency (now "Successor Agency"). Many of the other blocks of the DSP have since been 
developed. 
The DSP for Block 18 allows for: 
• 
1,007,897 square feet of retail, restaurant, and entertainment space (including a multi­
screen cinema); 
• 200 hotel rooms; 
• 322,000 square feet of office space; and 
• 292 residential units. 
The DSP designates Block 18, as a "commercial core district." The City and Successor 
Agency own some of the streets and developed land with parking facilities within Block 18. 
Otherwise, it is owned by private entities. There are existing agreements that describe the 
obligations of the parties relating to their interconnected ownership (easements, parking 
leases, etc.). The fonner redevelopment agency entered into a development agreement with a 
private developer to build the Town Center Project. 
Construction started in 2008. It was halted midway when the developer's financing 
failed. Recent court decisions have resolved pending litigation between private parties that 
kept the Project tied up. The parties are now moving forward to complete the project, which 
currently "half-constructed and donnant." 
In 20 I 0, the Successor Agency and project developer entered into the "20 I 0 
Amended Disposition and Development and Owner Participation Agreement" ("ADDOPA") 
that provided for the completion of construction of the project and on-going operational 
issues between the property owners. Among other things, the ADDOPA contains a provision 
that allows the developer to transfer its interest to a new developer, subject to the Successor 
Agency approving certain criteria. Now that litigation is resolved, Wells Fargo Bank (which 
acquired the property through foreclosure and is the current "developer" under the 


File No. A-15-226 
Page No. 3 
ADDOPA) will be selling the property. Because five years have passed since the ADDOPA 
was entered, certain provisions of the ADDOPA will need to be updated and modified. 
Councilmember Larsson owns a home that is approximately 863 feet from the 
boundary of Block 18, and approximately 169 feet from the boundary of the overall 
DSP. 
Councilmember Larsson previously requested advice to determine whether he 
may participate in decisions to approve a new developer for Block 18. We have provided 
advice pertaining to this project in the Borger Advice Letter, I-15-110. We concluded in 
that prior advice, "[T]he development's scope, intensity, and proximity will lead to 
significant impacts that will have a foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember 
Larsson's home ... Accordingly, Councilmember Larsson has a conflict in decisions related 
to the DSP and redevelopment of Block 18, and may not make, participate in making, or use 
his official position to influence these decisions." Councilmember Larsson was advised 
that he may, however, participate in choosing a developer for Block 18 "so long as it 
does not reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions approving the 
DSP, the development plan for Block 18, or any other decision from which he may be 
disqualified." 
Councilmember Larsson requests further clarification regarding other actions in 
which he may participate. When a new developer is selected. provisions of the 
ADDO PA related to timing and phasing of construction, construction financing, 
extension of time frames, ownership structure of housing units, and other project details 
will need to be reviewed and potentially revised. These changes would not modify the 
development regulations of Block 18. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official 
has an interest specified in Section 87103. 
A public official has a "financial interest" in a government decision, within the meaning of 
the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. 2 
We concluded in our July 28, 2015 advice that due to the ';scope, intensity, and proximity" 
of the Town Center Project to Councilmember Larsson's home, he has "a conflict in decisions 
related to the DSP and redevelopment of Block 18, and may not make, participate in making, or use 
his official position to influence these decisions."3 
2 Section 87103; Regulation I 8700(a). 
3 You stated in your request that "under the fonner rules Councilmember Larsson would not have had a 
conflict because his propeny is more than 500 feet away from the Project." Please note that the 500-foot rule under the 
prior conflict of interest regulations was a rebuttable presumption. Under the prior rule, where an official's propeny was 


File No. A-15-226 
Page No. 4 
You seek further advice regarding "timing and phasing of construction, construction 
financing, extension of time frames, ownership structure of housing units, and other project 
details [that] will need to be reviewed and potentially revised." 
Segmentation: 
Regulation 18706 identifies circumstances in which an agency may segment governmental 
decisions. In some cases, "some large, complex decisions may be segmented into separate decisions 
so that even if an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the series of decisions, he 
may still participate in other components in which he has no financial interest. ,,4 
Regulation 18706 requires that all of the following must apply: 
"( 1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into 
separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in which the official 
has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from the other 
decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and a final 
decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's participation in any 
way; and 
"( 4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, the 
disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or otherwise 
financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified." 
Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of interest is "inextricably interrelated" 
to another decision, the official will be required to disqualify from both decisions. Regulation 
18706(b) provides that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision wi11 
effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 
Decisions regarding "timing and phasing of construction'· appear to be implementation 
decisions that may be segmented so long as the decisions do not reopen, determine, affirm, 
more than 500 feet from the subject property, the effect was presumed not to be material. However, this presumption 
could be rebutted by specific circumstances regarding the decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the property, 
which made it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the property. Specific 
circumstances listed to rebut the "no materiality" presumption (similar to those analyzed in the current regulation) 
included: "(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real property in which the official has an 
economic interest; (B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic interest; (C) The character of 
the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise 
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood." (Former Regulation 18705 .2(b )( l ).) 
4 Whitman Advice Letter, No. A-14-186; citing Milich Advice Letter, No. 1-04-216, and Merkuloff Advice 
Letter, No. 1-90-542. 


File No. A-15-226 
Page No. 5 
nullify, or alter the previous decisions regarding approval of the DSP or development plan for 
Block 18. 
However, "construction financing, extension of time frames," and possibly even 
"ownership structure of housing units"5 appear to be decisions interlinked to approval of the 
DSP or development plan for Block 18. For instance, a significant aspect of approval of a 
project is "construction financing" or funding, which may determine whether development will 
move forward. A financing plan, and its approval or disapproval could result in the termination 
or a significant modification of the project as a whole.6 Similarly, "extension of time frames," 
or the failure to extend a project's time frame (as well as any significant delays) can also have 
profound consequences for a project approval. 7 We emphasize that this conclusion applies to 
all major policy decisions about the DSP and Block 18 that could determine, affirm, nullify, or 
alter the result of the initial DSP decision. 
You also ask whether the councilmember may participate in "other project details [that] 
will need to be reviewed and potentially revised." Councilmember Larsson must examine each 
decision regarding the DSP and Block 18 to determine whether the decisions are merely 
implementing decisions that may be separable from the project decisions as a whole. 
Without the factual context of a particular decision, we are unable to reach a conclusion 
regarding "other project details" that may be revised. You may wish to request written advice 
from us in the future when you have more facts or a specific pending decision. 
ER:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
Emelyn Rodriguez 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 0 
s We are uncertain about what the term "ownership structure of housing units" entails. For instance, if it 
detennines the types of uses for the high-density housing (e.g. apartment units versus condominium ownership), then it 
would also be an interlinked decision. 
6 Martello Advice Letter, A-92-471. 
1 McMurlry Advice Letter, A-95-377, Roach Advice Letter, 1-91-405; Sweeney Advice Letter, A-89-639.

Construction financing, extension of time frames, and possibly ownership structure of the 
housing units are decisions "inextricably interrelated" to the DSP and Block 18 determinations to 
of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 10 through 189 97 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. the extent that they reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter these previous decisions. 
Therefore, Councilmember Larsson will be required to disqualify from these decisions.

QUESTION: Under the Act, may Councilmember Larsson participate in decisions related to timing and 
phasing of construction, construction financing, extension of time frames, and ownership structure 
of housing units of the Sunnyvale Town Center Project ('"Town Center Project") located in Block 
18 of the Downtown Sunnyvale Specific Plan ("DSP")?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Larsson may participate in decisions related to timing and phasing of 
construction of the Town Center Project so long as these decisions do not have a foreseeable 
material financial effect on his financial interests, and so long as they do not reopen, determine, 
affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions approving the DSP, the development plan for Block 
18, or any other decision from which he may be disqualified. 
Construction financing, extension of time frames, and possibly ownership structure of the 
housing units are decisions "inextricably interrelated" to the DSP and Block 18 determinations to 
of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 10 through 189 97 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. the extent that they reopen, determine, affirm, nullify, or alter these previous decisions. 
Therefore, Councilmember Larsson will be required to disqualify from these decisions.
